planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist, deep, fertile to average loamy or sandy soil in full sun; Sitka spruce grows naturally in the moist Pacific coastal fog belt and requires high humidity and consistent moisture — it does not perform well in dry, continental climates. Excellent for coastal planting.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist, deep, fertile to average loamy or sandy soil in full sun; Sitka spruce is the largest spruce in the world and the third largest conifer — reaching up to 300 feet in the old-growth forests of the Pacific Northwest and Southeast Alaska. The Champion Tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park is over 300 feet tall. Native to the Pacific Coast fog belt from northern California to Alaska, it grows in the perpetually moist, fog-drip zone where rainfall can reach 200 inches per year. The characteristic very sharp, stiff needles and the flat, pendant branches create a distinctive large-scale landscape form. Sitka spruce wood has the highest strength-to-weight ratio of any wood and was the primary material for early aircraft construction — nearly all World War I aircraft frames and Howard Hughes's 'Spruce Goose' were built from it. One of the premier coastal windbreak trees where fog and ocean influence moderate summer drought.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy or sandy, moist to average soil in full sun; white spruce is one of the most widely adaptable and cold-hardy spruces, tolerating a wide range of soils including slightly alkaline conditions. More drought-tolerant than many spruces once established.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy or sandy, moist to average soil in full sun; white spruce is one of the dominant trees of the North American boreal forest — the vast coniferous forest stretching from coast to coast across Canada and Alaska. Extremely cold-hardy (to zone 2), it is often the northernmost tree species before the tundra takes over. The short, blue-green to gray-green needles have a distinctive cat-urine-like odor when crushed (hence 'cat spruce'). The popular dwarf cultivar 'Conica' (dwarf Alberta spruce) is one of the most widely planted landscape conifers in North America, producing a perfect, dense, cone-shaped form that needs no pruning. White spruce is widely used in the paper pulp industry and as a Christmas tree — particularly the cultivar 'Densata' (Black Hills spruce), which is considered the premium Christmas tree for the upper Midwest due to its density and excellent needle retention.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; balsam fir grows naturally in cold, moist coniferous and mixed forests of the Northeast, requiring cool, moist conditions — it does not tolerate heat or dry soils. Best for zones 3–5.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; balsam fir is the Christmas tree of the Northeast — the sweet, clean, resinous fragrance of balsam fir needles is the quintessential Christmas scent, and the tree holds its needles exceptionally well after cutting. The species forms the upper forest zone of the Appalachians from New England to the peaks of the Smoky Mountains, and the classic boreal forest landscape of northern Canada. Balsam sap collected from bark blisters was historically used as a waterproof cement, medicinally as a wound treatment, and in optical instruments as 'Canada balsam' for mounting microscope slides. The flat needles with their two distinct white stomatal bands (underside) are characteristic of firs. Short-lived in the landscape (40–70 years) compared to other conifers, and best in the cool, moist conditions of the upper Midwest and New England.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, moist, slightly acidic, fertile loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; Fraser fir is native to the high-elevation spruce-fir forests of the southern Appalachians (above 4,500 feet) and requires cool, moist conditions. Does not tolerate heat or prolonged drought.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, moist, slightly acidic, fertile loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; Fraser fir is widely considered the finest Christmas tree in the United States — with a perfect, dense, symmetrical cone shape, outstanding needle retention after cutting, pleasant fragrance, and stiff branches capable of holding heavy ornaments without drooping. The species is native to the high-elevation spruce-fir forests of the southern Appalachians (primarily in western North Carolina, eastern Tennessee, and southwest Virginia above 5,000 feet) where it faces severe threat from the introduced balsam woolly adelgid (Adelges piceae), which has killed a large proportion of wild mature trees. Christmas tree farming in the Carolina mountains (particularly around Ashe County, NC) has made Fraser fir a major agricultural crop — North Carolina produces more Christmas trees than any other state. More heat-tolerant than balsam fir, succeeding in zone 7 where balsam fir fails.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; white fir is the most drought-tolerant of the native North American firs, adapting to drier, more continental conditions than other firs. More tolerant of heat and drought stress than Engelmann spruce or balsam fir.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; white fir is among the most elegant and garden-worthy of the native North American firs — notable for its long, curved, distinctively blue-gray to blue-green needles that are arranged in a beautifully symmetrical pattern on each branch. The blue-gray form (sometimes called 'concolor fir' in the nursery trade) has striking, ice-blue foliage that rivals Colorado blue spruce in color intensity but with a much more graceful habit. White fir is significantly more drought tolerant than most firs, native to the drier mountain forests of the Sierra Nevada, Rocky Mountains, and Great Basin from Colorado to southern California. The Christmas tree industry uses white fir as an alternative to Fraser and balsam firs in western states, and it is one of the premier ornamental firs for large landscape settings in the West. Relatively more tolerant of dry summers than other firs.
